topics.heading: Project Introduction
points: JioOnePay is a **payment aggregation platform** by Jio Platforms — merchants integrate once with us instead of separately with every payment gateway or bank.

topics.heading: Business Problem
points: Before aggregators, merchants integrated individually with every gateway — high effort, high complexity. JioOnePay is the single integration point that handles routing, processing, and response for them.

topics.heading: High-Level Flow
points: Customer picks a payment method (UPI / Card / Wallet / Net Banking) on a merchant app.
Merchant sends the request to JioOnePay via REST APIs.
Platform validates it and routes it to the right payment gateway/bank.
Response (plus status updates, refunds, monitoring) flows back to the merchant.

topics.heading: Application Architecture
points: **Microservices architecture** — each service is independently deployable, scalable, and easy to extend.
**API Gateway pattern** — `jop-gateway-service` is the single entry point for all traffic; it routes requests to the right microservice and handles resilience (circuit breaker, rate limiting).

topics.heading: Technologies Used
points: **Backend** — Core Java, Spring Boot, Spring Data JPA, Oracle Database, REST APIs
**Frontend** — React, Next.js, TypeScript

topics.heading: Key Features
points: Merchant Onboarding
Payment Gateway Integration
Multiple Payment Modes (UPI, Card, Wallet, Net Banking)
Dynamic Payment Routing
Refunds & Transaction Monitoring

topics.heading: My Roles & Responsibilities
points: Building REST APIs & business logic in Spring Boot, integrating new payment gateways, writing optimized Oracle SQL.
Frontend development in React/Next.js — building UI components and wiring them to backend APIs.
Code reviews, unit tests, debugging, and working with QA/Product/DevOps through release.

topics.heading: Challenges
points: **Problem** — Integrating multiple payment gateways, where each gateway had different request/response formats, authentication mechanisms, and APIs.
**Solution** — Implemented a common abstraction layer that converts our internal request into the gateway-specific format and transforms the gateway response back into our standard response model.
**Impact** — Merchants use a single API, and new gateways can be integrated without impacting existing merchants.

topics.heading: Outcome
points: Working on JioOnePay has given me hands-on experience building enterprise-scale payment systems, developing RESTful microservices, integrating third-party payment gateways, optimizing SQL queries, handling production issues, and working in a distributed microservices environment.

topics.heading: Overview
points: **jop-gateway-service** is the single entry point for all JioOnePay traffic — an **API Gateway pattern** implementation. It only routes requests and protects the system (circuit breaker, rate limit, bulkhead); no business logic, no database.
Nothing is hardcoded — routes and resilience limits live in Zookeeper as JSON and refresh live, no restart needed.
Runs on **port 9985**.

topics.heading: Tech Stack (from pom.xml)
points: **Core** — Spring Boot 3.1.5, Java 17, Spring Cloud 2022.0.2
**Gateway engine** — Spring Cloud Gateway (WebFlux + Netty, reactive, non-blocking)
**Config** — Spring Cloud Zookeeper Config — pulls routes + resilience limits from Zookeeper
**Resilience** — Resilience4j (Circuit Breaker, Rate Limiter, Bulkhead, Time Limiter)
**Observability** — Log4j2, Micrometer + Prometheus, Brave tracing

topics.heading: How Routing Works
points: All routes live in one Zookeeper key, `config-properties` (Base64 JSON) — each service's id, real URL, and path mappings.
`ApiRoutesLocatorService` builds the in-memory route table from this JSON on every lookup — no restart needed when routes change.
`ZookeeperRefreshListener` auto-refreshes the config when Zookeeper data changes.
**Important:** no Consul/Kubernetes discovery — target URLs are pre-configured directly in Zookeeper.

topics.heading: Request Flow (simple diagram)
topics.imageAlt: jop-gateway-service architecture diagram showing client to gateway to resilience filters to downstream service or fallback
points: Client → Gateway (:9985) → route match → path rewrite.
Circuit Breaker, Rate Limiter, and Bulkhead each check the call, in that order.
All checks pass → forwarded to the real service URL, response returned as-is.
Any check fails → redirected to `/fallback`, which returns a clean JSON error instead of a raw one.

topics.heading: Resilience Settings
points: Circuit Breaker, Rate Limiter, Bulkhead, and Time Limiter can each be tuned **per route** (or fall back to global defaults) via a second Zookeeper key, `resilience-properties`.
Circuit Breaker fallback only triggers on HTTP 500/502/503/504. Rate Limiter and Bulkhead reject immediately with their own error message — they don't go through `/fallback`.

topics.heading: Overview
points: JioOnePay is a **synchronous, REST-only** platform — every call, internal or external, is a direct HTTP request/response. There is no message broker (no Kafka, no RabbitMQ) anywhere in the ~60 microservices checked.
Internal calls (service-to-service) and external calls (to banks/payment gateways) use **two completely different HTTP client approaches** — that split is the main thing to remember.

topics.heading: Internal — Service to Service
points: **Spring Cloud OpenFeign** is the client — a declarative REST client: you write a Java interface annotated `@FeignClient(name = "service-name")`, call it like a local method, and Feign builds the actual HTTP call underneath.
The `name` isn't a hardcoded URL — it's resolved at call-time via **Zookeeper-based service discovery**.
Example: `jop-core-service` calls `jop-payment-s2s-service`, the wallet service, and `jop-core-pg-details-service` — each through its own `@FeignClient` interface.
**Spring Retry** (`@Retryable`) is layered on top of some Feign clients for transient failures — e.g. a 3-second backoff on the PG-config client.
**Important:** these calls go **directly** service-to-service — they do **not** pass through `jop-gateway-service`. The gateway is only for external, client-facing traffic.

topics.heading: External — Banks & Payment Gateways
points: Each bank/PG integration is its own microservice (`jop-core-razorpay-service`, `jop-core-citi-service`, `jop-core-phonepe-service`, etc.), and each one writes its **own Apache HttpClient 5 wrapper class** to call that PG's API.
There's **no shared HTTP client abstraction across PGs** — Razorpay's wrapper and Citi's wrapper are separate, independently-written classes, not implementations of one common interface.

**mTLS (mutual TLS)** is used for bank-grade integrations — e.g. Citi loads a **PKCS12 client certificate** over **TLS 1.2**, so the bank verifies the caller's identity too, not just the caller trusting the bank.
Timeouts, proxy URL/port, and certificate file paths are all pulled from **Zookeeper config** — so they can be tuned per environment without a redeploy.

topics.heading: Why No Message Broker
points: Every request is synchronous end-to-end — simple to reason about and easy to trace one request through the whole chain, but it means a slow downstream call (e.g. a bank taking long to respond) blocks that entire chain.
This is exactly why the **Circuit Breaker / Bulkhead / Rate Limiter** layer exists at the gateway — to contain that blast radius since there's no async buffer (queue) to fall back on.

topics.heading: Shared Infrastructure
points: **`jop-common-redis-lib`** — a shared Redis client used across services for caching and shared state (e.g. idempotency keys, temporary session data). It's for **caching, not messaging**.
**`jop-common-lib`** — the shared base library that pulls in Feign, Zookeeper config, and Spring Retry, so each service doesn't configure these from scratch.
